content.text: Threat Assessment Division, statting desk. Two minutes per asset. The field does not wait for committee.

Build an enemy in four picks: Tier → Body → Role → 1–2 abilities. The tier chassis does the arithmetic, the body decides which damage math runs, the role bends the chassis. Statting an enemy takes two minutes, and two minutes is the budget.
The tier chassis
Enemies attack with the same grammar players do: accuracy die + bonus vs Evasion (see Attacks & the Shell Clock).
Body: Flesh, Hide, Shell
The taxonomy and the damage pipeline are player-facing rules — they live on Attacks & the Shell Clock. GM-side, the body pick is what it does to your prep:

Flesh — straight HP, the fastest math in the game, and the most common profile in the world: rift fauna, margin gangs, swarms. Give fauna roughly 1.5× humanoid HP for its tier — high HP is the whole defense.
Hide — Value only, and it never breaks. Hide runs 1–4. Penetration deletes it; Pierce finds no gaps in muscle; light blades don't grind here, they just lose.

Shell — the full pipeline, and it breaks. Armored humanoids wear the player armor ladder (see Armor); constructs and chitin print their own shell numbers in the Bestiary. A shell cracking mid-fight is a free phase change.
Which weapons beat which bodies is the doctrine matrix, printed for players on Weapons.
Roles
A role modifies the chassis and hands the enemy its 1–2 abilities.

rows: Fights from Long–Extreme bands; Stable-Platform behavior — it anchors and shoots
One zone or condition verb (see Conditions)
Heals or buffs others — kill the voice

content.label: DESIGN GAP
content.text: The four-pick procedure ends with “1–2 abilities from the role menu,” but no per-role ability menu is printed in the Phase 7 docs. The chassis modifiers above and the Bestiary's signature lines are everything that exists.
Activations, not bodies
Encounters are budgeted in activations, not bodies. Hordes are how the room stays full without the clock drowning:
4–6 fodder compress into one unit: shared HP pool, one activation.
Attack dice scale with surviving members.
Automatic break at 50% casualties — no roll.
The room stays full; the clock stays fluid.

content.label: DESIGN GAP
content.text: No activation budget is printed — nothing in the Phase 7 docs says how many enemy activations a given party size or difficulty buys. Bosses print their own action economy (see Bosses); everything below them is unguided.
Enemy reactions
Enemies use the chassis RP column and bank AP exactly like players (RP rules — see Turn, AP & Reactions). Fodder never reacts — fodder is for movement.
Break lines — fear as arithmetic
Every creature block carries one Break line: the condition where its survival math flips. There is no morale subsystem — the line is the whole rule.
When the line triggers, the creature rolls its Resilience Check vs Standard 8 (check grammar — see The Two Rolls). Fodder skips the roll — fodder just breaks.
Gangs scatter at casualties.
Wounded big game turns desperate before it flees.
Corp professionals break by doctrine — withdrawal the moment the mission stops pricing.
Constructs have no line at all.
Influence and Sin Eater verbs key off Break lines (see Influence, Sin Eater) — Surrender Demand-class play has a hook in every block.
The profile card

Every enemy prints as five lines: Stat · Signature · Break · Carve · Graft. What it rolls, what it does, when it stops, and what the body is worth. The full launch catalog is the Bestiary; carve yields and clocks live on Carving; graft installation lives on Mutations & Grafts. Every entry feeds the economy — the party kills it, the Carver prices it.
Threat profiles are estimates prepared from available data. Sentinel reminds field personnel that an accurately priced opponent is an opponent already half paid for.
